title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Quantitative Research
Quantitative research is centered on numerical data and statistical analysis. It seeks to identify patterns, relationships, and trends that can be generalized across populations.
Characteristics: structured, objective, replicable, uses statistical inference.
Common methods: surveys and questionnaires, experiments, secondary data analysis.






NoteSecondary Data Analysis in Intelligence



Much intelligence analysis is secondary data analysis by definition — analysts rarely collect their own data from scratch. Understanding how existing data was collected, cleaned, and coded is therefore essential before drawing conclusions from it.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Qualitative Research
Qualitative research seeks to understand meanings, experiences, and social processes through non-numerical data. It is often exploratory and context-sensitive.
Characteristics: flexible, inductive, produces depth over breadth.
Common methods: interviews, focus groups, case studies — in-depth examination of a single unit to understand broader dynamics.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Mixed-Methods Research


Sequential Designs
One method informs the next. For example: qualitative interviews identify themes → quantitative survey tests those themes at scale.


Concurrent Designs
Both methods run simultaneously and findings are integrated during analysis, producing a holistic picture that neither alone could achieve.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Crawling, Indexing, and Ranking
Search engines operate through three core processes:

Crawling — automated bots traverse the web following hyperlinks, discovering pages
Indexing — discovered content is analyzed, parsed, and stored in the search engine’s database
Ranking — when a query is submitted, the engine scores indexed pages against relevance algorithms







TipAdvanced Search Operators — Practice



Try these in your browser:

filetype:xlsx Russia economic indicators — find Excel files on Russian economics
site:gov "intelligence analysis" — limit results to .gov domains
"climate change" after:2023 — restrict to recent publications
intitle:"annual report" OPEC — find pages with “annual report” in the title

These techniques apply directly to OSINT collection tasks.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Thought Exercise: Applied Search
Working in groups, devise a search strategy for each of the following:

A database (Excel format) on Russian economic indicators for the last 30 years
A PDF version of a recent article by a JMU intelligence analysis faculty member
Recent news articles related to JMU football published in the last 24 hours
Regional disparities in American concern about climate change
Research articles published in Portugal on artificial intelligence

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Operationalization
Operationalization is the process of defining how an abstract concept will be measured in concrete, observable terms.








Abstract Concept
Possible Operationalization
Limitation




State instability
Anti-government demonstrations per year
Misses rural unrest; biased toward media-covered events


Military capability
Defense spending as % of GDP
Ignores efficiency, technology quality, training


Popular support
Survey approval ratings
Survey conditions may not reflect genuine opinion


Radicalization
Attendance at extremist events
Underestimates online radicalization









NoteThe Operationalization Problem in Intelligence



Many of the concepts most important to intelligence — resolve, intent, morale, legitimacy — resist clean operationalization. Analysts often use proxy indicators: observable behaviors that correlate with the unmeasurable concept. The key discipline is being explicit about what your proxy does and does not capture.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Unit of Analysis
The unit of analysis is the entity about which you are making claims. Common units in intelligence analysis:

Individual — persons of interest, leaders, operatives
Organization — armed groups, companies, government agencies
Event — attacks, protests, elections, financial transactions
State/country — the most common unit in quantitative IR







WarningThe Ecological Fallacy



Drawing conclusions about individuals from aggregate data is the ecological fallacy. If a country has high per capita income and high support for a political movement, it does not follow that wealthy individuals drive that support. Always match the level of your data to the level of your claim.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Levels of Analysis








Level
Focus
Example Question




Individual
Leaders, key decision-makers
Does Putin’s risk tolerance differ from his advisors’?


Organizational
Bureaucratic/institutional factors
How does IRGC organizational culture affect operational decisions?


State
Domestic political, economic, military factors
Does regime type predict likelihood of WMD pursuit?


Systemic
Structure of the international system
Does unipolarity reduce great-power conflict?

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Variable Types



Dependent Variable (DV)

The outcome you are trying to explain or predict.

Independent Variable (IV)

The factor you believe causes or predicts variation in the DV.

Control Variable

A factor held constant to isolate the IV–DV relationship.

Confounding Variable

A third variable that affects both IV and DV, creating a spurious appearance of causation.

Mediating Variable

A variable through which the IV affects the DV.










TipDiscussion: Operationalizing Threat



In small groups, choose one of the following questions. Identify: (a) the DV and how to measure it, (b) the primary IV and how to operationalize it, (c) at least one likely confounder, and (d) the most significant limitation of your operationalization.

Do states facing existential threats pursue nuclear weapons more frequently?
Does social media use increase the likelihood of mass protests?
Does foreign economic aid reduce armed conflict in recipient countries?

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
What is Systems Thinking?
Systems thinking is an analytical approach that views complex phenomena as systems—sets of interconnected, interdependent components whose interactions produce emergent behavior that cannot be understood by examining parts in isolation.
Rather than asking “What causes X?”, systems thinking asks “How do the relationships among multiple variables produce the pattern we observe in X?”






NoteRelevance to Hypothesis Development



When you identify variables for a model, you are making implicit claims about what matters and what does not. Systems thinking forces you to be explicit about those claims—and to consider feedback loops and unintended consequences that purely linear thinking misses.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Key Principles of Systems Thinking



Interconnectedness

Every element of a system is connected to other elements through relationships. Changes in one variable propagate through the system. Analyzing any single variable in isolation produces an incomplete picture.

Feedback Loops

Closed causal chains where an effect loops back to influence its own cause. There are two types (see below).

Reinforcing (Positive) Feedback Loops

Change in one direction amplifies further change in the same direction. Not inherently positive in valence—can represent escalation, arms races, or cascading instability. Example: military spending increases → perceived threat increases → adversary military spending increases → perceived threat increases further.

Balancing (Negative) Feedback Loops

System-level resistance to change; the loop pushes back against deviation from a target state. Example: high inflation → interest rate increases → borrowing decreases → economic activity slows → inflation decreases.

Non-linearity

Relationships in complex systems are often non-linear—a small change in an input may have a large effect on an output (tipping points), or a large change may have negligible effect (thresholds). Linear models cannot capture these dynamics.

Emergence

System-level properties that are not present in any individual component. The whole behaves differently than the sum of its parts. Terrorist network resilience is an emergent property—no single node produces it.

Delayed Effects

Effects may manifest long after their cause. Sanctions imposed today may not produce intended economic effects for months or years. Models that ignore time lags will misattribute causation.

Holistic View

Analysis must encompass the whole system, not just the most visible or accessible parts. Focusing only on measurable variables creates blind spots.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
Building a Causal Loop Diagram (CLD)
A causal loop diagram is a visual representation of the causal relationships and feedback loops within a system. It does not quantify relationships—it maps their direction and structure.
Step-by-step process:



Step 1: Define the System

State the key problem or question you are exploring. What are the boundaries of the system? What outcomes are you trying to explain?

Step 2: Identify Key Variables

List and categorize the variables relevant to your system. Are they independent, dependent, or intermediate? What scale and type are they?

Step 3: Establish Causal Links

For each pair of related variables, draw an arrow from cause to effect. Label each arrow: (+) if an increase in the cause produces an increase in the effect; (-) if an increase in the cause produces a decrease in the effect.

Step 4: Identify Feedback Loops

Trace closed loops in your diagram. Count the number of negative links: an even number (including zero) indicates a reinforcing loop; an odd number indicates a balancing loop.

Step 5: Draw the Diagram

List all variables, then add arrows and signs. Clarity and legibility matter—a cluttered CLD is as useless as a cluttered chart.

Step 6: Review and Adjust

Are there gaps? Are feedback loops internally consistent? Have you included all major drivers and consequences? Have independent reviewers examine the diagram.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
section: Benefits of Systems Thinking for Analysis
text:
Benefits of Systems Thinking for Analysis


Better Variable Identification
Systems thinking forces analysts to think beyond obvious first-order causes. A system diagram of “nuclear proliferation” might reveal that domestic politics, alliance commitments, and regime security concerns interact with the technical capability variables that tend to dominate quantitative models.
Enhanced Predictive Power
By mapping how variables interact—including delayed effects and feedback loops—analysts can anticipate how a change in one variable will ripple through the system. Interventions that appear effective in isolation may be counterproductive at the system level.


Better Decision-Making
Systems thinking highlights potential unintended consequences before they occur. Sanctions designed to reduce a regime’s capability may strengthen its internal cohesion through a rally-around-the-flag effect—a balancing loop that counteracts the intended effect.
More Robust Hypotheses
Hypotheses derived from systems thinking are more likely to specify the mechanism connecting variables, not just the correlation. Mechanistic specificity is a hallmark of analytically rigorous hypothesis development.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
section: Case 1: Sanctions Against North Korea — A Feedback Loop Analysis
text:
Case 1: Sanctions Against North Korea — A Feedback Loop Analysis
Sanctions imposed on North Korea to compel denuclearization have operated for decades with limited observable effect on North Korea’s weapons programs. Systems thinking explains why. The causal structure contains multiple feedback loops that counteract the intended effect of economic coercion.
Intended mechanism (the linear assumption): > Sanctions → Economic pain → Regime capitulation → Denuclearization
What systems analysis reveals:


Reinforcing loop — Rally-around-the-flag
Sanctions-induced economic hardship → Regime frames hardship as foreign aggression → Domestic nationalism increases → Regime legitimacy strengthens → Hardline position becomes domestically necessary to sustain
This reinforcing loop amplifies regime cohesion in direct response to the pressure intended to weaken it. Empirical studies on North Korea have found that periods of intensified sanctions correspond with increased domestic propaganda output, not capitulation signals.


Balancing loop — Elite defection risk
Sanctions → Economic contraction → Elite material wellbeing threatened → Elite defection or coup risk → Regime redistributes resources to core loyalists → Economic contraction among population deepens, but regime stability is maintained
This balancing loop absorbs the potential political threat from sanctions by concentrating resources within the elite network—precisely the opposite of the intended distributional effect.








NoteAnalytical Takeaway



A linear model of sanctions predicts capitulation. A systems model predicts adaptation and consolidation—which is what the empirical record shows. Before recommending or evaluating a coercive policy instrument, mapping the full causal structure (including second- and third-order loops) is essential analytical due diligence.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
section: Case 2: The 2003 Iraq Occupation — Institutional Collapse as a System Failure
text:
Case 2: The 2003 Iraq Occupation — Institutional Collapse as a System Failure
The postwar period in Iraq demonstrated how policy interventions can trigger cascading system failures through mechanisms that linear planning does not anticipate.
The critical decision: Coalition Provisional Authority Order No. 2 (May 2003) disbanded the Iraqi Army, removing approximately 400,000 armed men from institutional employment overnight.
System consequences:



Phase 1: Initial shock

Army dissolution → immediate loss of income and status for 400,000 individuals with weapons training → security vacuum as the primary order-providing institution disappears

Phase 2: Grievance amplification (reinforcing loop)

Unemployed soldiers → economic desperation + institutional grievance → insurgent recruitment becomes easy and cheap → insurgency capability increases

Phase 3: Sectarian activation

Security vacuum → Shia militias and Kurdish peshmerga expand to fill void → Sunni population perceives ethnic threat → Sunni fighters join insurgency as both economic actors and sectarian defenders (two independent recruitment pathways reinforcing each other)

Phase 4: External involvement

Instability → Al-Qaeda in Iraq enters → foreign fighters provide financing and ideological framing → insurgency becomes internationalized → coalition military response intensifies → civilian casualties → insurgent recruitment further increases (another reinforcing loop)




The system dynamics perspective reveals that the insurgency was not primarily caused by the presence of foreign fighters or by “bad actors” who could have been identified in advance — it was produced by the institutional architecture of the intervention itself. A systems analysis conducted before Order No. 2 could have identified these cascading pathways.

title: Week 4: Research Design and Systems Thinking
text:
When Systems Shift Suddenly
Some of the most important outcomes in international security are characterized by sudden, discontinuous change — the collapse of a regime that appeared stable, the rapid escalation of a conflict that seemed contained, the sudden spread of a protest movement. Systems thinking offers a framework for understanding why these transitions happen.
Key concept: tipping points and bifurcations
In a system with strong balancing loops, a variable (say, regime stability) may remain near its equilibrium despite perturbations — the system “absorbs” small shocks. But once a critical threshold is crossed, the system reorganizes abruptly. The balancing loops that maintained the old equilibrium weaken or reverse; reinforcing loops that were previously dominated begin to dominate. The system bifurcates to a new state.
Illustrative cases:









Phenomenon
Pre-tip Dynamics
Tipping Mechanism
Post-tip Dynamics




Arab Spring 2010–11
Authoritarian stability through repression (balancing loop)
Mohamed Bouazizi self-immolation → social media amplification of grievance → cost of protest fell below cost of silence
Reinforcing loop: protests → government crackdowns → more protests; cascade across regional states


Soviet collapse 1989–91
Ideological legitimacy + military deterrence maintained stability
Gorbachev’s signals that force would not be used → cost of defection fell for Eastern European elites
Cascade: each defection reduced the expected cost of the next; the system reorganized faster than anyone predicted


Financial contagion 2008
Mortgage-backed securities held by globally interconnected financial institutions
Lehman Brothers failure → counterparty uncertainty → credit markets freeze
Reinforcing loop: credit freeze → institutional failures → more uncertainty → deeper freeze









WarningThe Prediction Problem



Tipping points are extraordinarily difficult to predict precisely because the systems that generate them appear stable until they are not. Leading indicators exist — accelerating protest frequency before regime collapse, rising volatility before financial crisis — but threshold identification requires continuous system monitoring, not periodic snapshot analysis. Intelligence that reports on what a system is without modeling where it is relative to its thresholds will miss the discontinuity.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text: Analytical work requires two foundational capabilities: understanding how data is structured and stored, and knowing how to retrieve and manipulate it computationally. This lecture covers scales of measurement, database structure, proxy variables, and data quality — the structural foundations — then introduces the computational tools that operationalize those concepts: SQL for querying relational databases and APIs for programmatic data collection.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
The Five Scales




Nominal Scale

An unordered set of categories identified only by name or label. Numbers, if used, have no mathematical meaning. You can only determine sameness or difference. Examples: country names, ethnic group, religious affiliation, vehicle type. Valid operations: counting, mode, frequency tables, chi-square tests.



Ordinal Scale

An ordered set of categories. You know the direction of difference (A > B) but not the magnitude — the intervals between ranks are not assumed equal. Examples: military rank (Private < Corporal < Sergeant), education level, Likert survey responses (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree), threat level (Low / Medium / High). Valid operations: median, percentiles, rank-order correlations (Spearman’s rho).



Interval Scale

An ordered scale with equal intervals between values, but no true zero point. Zero is arbitrary — it does not mean “absence” of the attribute. Examples: temperature in Celsius or Fahrenheit (0°C does not mean “no temperature”), IQ scores, calendar years. Valid operations: mean, standard deviation, Pearson correlation. Ratios are not valid (40°C is not “twice as hot” as 20°C).



Ratio Scale

An interval scale with a true, meaningful zero — zero indicates complete absence of the attribute. All arithmetic operations including ratios are valid. Examples: weight, income, distance, duration, number of casualties, GDP in dollars.



Binary Scale

A special case of nominal data with exactly two values, often coded 0/1. Examples: treaty membership (Yes/No), conflict onset (1 = conflict this year, 0 = no conflict), gender in binary operationalizations. Also called dummy variables in regression analysis.











TipA Quick Memory Aid: NOIR



Nominal → Ordinal → Interval → Ratio. Each level includes the properties of the one before it. Ratio data gives you the most information; nominal gives you the least.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
What is a Database?
A database is an organized, structured collection of data that enables efficient storage, retrieval, updating, and management. Every IC data platform — from classified warehouses to commercial analytics tools like Palantir — is built on relational database principles.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Tables, Fields, and Records
Every relational database is built from tables:








Concept
Definition
Analogy




Table
A collection of related data organized in rows and columns
A spreadsheet tab


Field (Column)
A single data attribute; the variable being recorded
A column header


Record (Row)
A single observation or entry
One row of data









NoteThe “Tall Rectangle” Rule



Well-structured databases are tall (many rows, fewer columns), not wide (few rows, many columns). Each row is one observation; each column is one variable. Violating this — putting “Year 2020 GDP,” “Year 2021 GDP,” “Year 2022 GDP” as three separate columns — makes joining, querying, and analysis far more difficult. This is the tidy data principle.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Database Keys


Primary Key
A field (or combination of fields) that uniquely identifies each record in a table. Requirements: - Must be unique — no two rows can share the same value - Cannot be NULL (empty) - Should be stable — should not change over time
Examples: Student ID, UN country code, a system-generated UUID.


Foreign Key
A field in one table that references the primary key in another table. Foreign keys enforce referential integrity — you cannot create a record referencing an entity that does not exist in the parent table.
Purpose: links tables together, enabling relational queries. A “Conflict Events” table with a country_id foreign key links to a “Countries” table, enabling country-level aggregation.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Structuring Data for Analysis
Best practices when building or receiving a dataset:

Use unique identifiers in the first column
Normalize data: break related information into logical separate tables rather than repeating values
Use consistent naming conventions — never mix USA, U.S., and United States in the same column
Use appropriate data types — store numbers as numbers, dates as dates, not as text
Document every field in a data dictionary (see Part X)

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
What is a Proxy Variable?
A proxy variable is a variable used in place of one that cannot be directly measured. Many of the most important concepts in intelligence — state intentions, regime stability, radicalization, military readiness — cannot be directly observed. Analysts must substitute measurable indicators.






NoteExamples of Proxy Variables in IA










Concept (Unobservable)
Proxy Variable (Observable)




Nuclear weapons capability
Number of centrifuges; plutonium production reactor capacity


State instability
GDP per capita growth, infant mortality rate, Fragile States Index score


Regime intent to proliferate
Imports of dual-use materials; missile test frequency


Social media radicalization
Proportion of followers linked to designated extremist accounts


Military readiness
Training exercise frequency; logistics vehicle activity in satellite imagery

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Cautions in Using Proxies
When using proxy variables, always:

Justify the connection — explain theoretically why your proxy measures the underlying concept
Acknowledge the gap — the proxy is not the concept; be explicit about what it misses
Check for alternative explanations — could your proxy be driven by something other than the concept you care about?
Use multiple proxies — triangulation across several imperfect indicators is stronger than reliance on one

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Why Is Data “Dirty”?
Real-world data is almost never clean. It arrives from multiple sources, collected at different times, by different people, using different standards. The preprocessing and cleaning step — often 60–80% of total project time — is where analytical quality is won or lost. Errors introduced here compound through every subsequent step.



Missing Values

Fields with no recorded value. Can occur because the data was never collected, was collected but lost, is being withheld, or represents a genuine non-occurrence. How missing values are handled is one of the most consequential methodological choices in any analysis.

Duplicate Records

The same entity or event appearing more than once in the dataset, usually as a result of merging data from multiple sources. Duplicates inflate counts and distort averages.

Inconsistent Coding

The same concept encoded differently across records: “USA,” “U.S.A.,” “United States,” “us.” Even minor inconsistencies break joins and aggregations.

Measurement Error

Recorded values that differ from true values due to instrument error, rounding, transcription mistakes, or deliberate falsification. State-reported economic data and casualty counts are common examples.

Structural Zeros

A value of zero that is not missing but represents a genuine absence. A dataset of conflict events may show zero events in a country not because data is unavailable, but because no events occurred.

Outliers

Extreme values that may represent genuine anomalies, data entry errors, or natural variation. Require investigation before any decision about handling.










WarningThe Intelligence-Specific Data Quality Problem



In intelligence contexts, data quality problems are often not random — they are the result of deliberate deception (adversaries reporting false numbers), selection bias (data only exists where collection infrastructure exists), or classification (the most critical information may be unavailable). These systematic biases cannot be corrected through statistical imputation. They must be reasoned about explicitly.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: Not All Missing Data Is the Same
text:
Not All Missing Data Is the Same
The statistical literature identifies three distinct mechanisms for missing data, each with different implications for how it should be handled:



Missing Completely at Random (MCAR)

The probability that a value is missing is unrelated to any variable in the dataset — not to the missing value itself, nor to other observed values. Example: a random sample of survey responses lost in a server crash. MCAR is the most benign assumption; listwise deletion (removing incomplete cases) does not introduce bias, though it reduces sample size.

Missing at Random (MAR)

The probability of being missing is related to observed variables, but not to the missing value itself, after conditioning on those variables. Example: older survey respondents are less likely to answer questions about internet use, but among respondents of the same age, missingness is random. Multiple imputation or maximum likelihood estimation can handle MAR data without serious bias.

Missing Not at Random (MNAR)

The probability of being missing depends on the missing value itself. Example: states with the highest human rights violations are the least likely to allow independent human rights monitoring — so the countries with the worst records are precisely the ones missing from the dataset. This is the most serious case; no statistical procedure can fully correct for MNAR without modeling the missing data mechanism explicitly.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
The Intelligence Application






WarningMNAR is the Default Assumption for Intelligence Data



In intelligence analysis, the assumption that data is MCAR or even MAR is almost always wrong. The most sensitive facilities are the least surveilled. The most dangerous individuals take the most precautions to avoid detection. The most repressive governments produce the least reliable official statistics. Data that is systematically missing because of the very phenomenon you are studying is the rule, not the exception. Analysts who impute missing values as if they were MCAR introduce exactly the kind of bias that makes analysis misleading precisely in the highest-stakes cases.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Common Handling Approaches (and their limits)









Approach
Assumption Required
When Appropriate
Risks




Listwise deletion
MCAR
When missing data is truly random and sample is large
Biased if MAR or MNAR; reduces power


Mean/median imputation
MCAR
Exploratory work only
Artificially reduces variance; distorts distributions


Multiple imputation
MAR
When missingness can be predicted from observed variables
Cannot correct MNAR


Model the missingness
MNAR explicitly modeled
When you have a theory of why values are missing
Complex; requires strong assumptions


Sensitivity analysis
Any
When you are uncertain about the mechanism
Tests whether conclusions are robust to alternative assumptions

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: The Most Value-Adding Step in Analytical Work
text:
The Most Value-Adding Step in Analytical Work
Data integration — combining information from multiple sources into a unified analytical dataset — is where the greatest analytical value is created and where the greatest analytical errors are introduced. No single source answers complex intelligence questions. The ability to merge, align, and reconcile disparate datasets is a core analytical skill.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Integration in the Intelligence Community
The IC operates from a collection philosophy of all-source analysis: HUMINT, SIGINT, IMINT, GEOINT, OSINT, and MASINT all provide partial pictures that only make sense when fused. The transition from single-source reporting to all-source assessment is the integration problem in its most consequential form.
At the data level, integration requires:

A common key to link records across datasets (country code, person identifier, geographic coordinate, date)
Consistent units and scales (all monetary figures in the same currency and year; all dates in the same format)
Temporal alignment (data collected at different frequencies or with different lags requires aggregation or interpolation)
Level-of-analysis compatibility (country-level data cannot be merged with individual-level data without aggregation decisions that embed analytical judgment)

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Why Integration Is Difficult



No Common Key

Datasets from different organizations often lack a shared unique identifier. Matching “Abu Ahmad al-Iraqi” across three databases that each use a different transliteration scheme and ID system is a non-trivial entity resolution problem.

Definitional Divergence

Two datasets measuring “conflict events” may define them differently — one includes protests, the other does not. Merging them requires reconciling the definitional gap, which is an analytical judgment, not a technical procedure.

Temporal Misalignment

ACLED releases data weekly; UCDP annually; World Bank GDP estimates may lag by 18 months. Integrating these requires decisions about what time period each variable represents.

Level-of-Analysis Mismatch

Merging individual-level survey data with country-level economic statistics requires aggregating one or disaggregating the other — each approach discards information and risks the ecological fallacy.

Entity Resolution at Scale

When integrating large datasets, the same real-world entity may appear under dozens of name variants, typos, or aliases. Automated fuzzy matching (edit distance, phonetic matching) helps but always introduces false positives and false negatives.

Provenance Loss

Once data from multiple sources is merged, the trail back to original sources becomes harder to maintain. A merged dataset with no provenance documentation cannot be audited, replicated, or corrected.

Changing Coverage

A dataset that covered 50 countries in 2005 and 80 in 2020 will show apparent change that reflects coverage expansion, not real-world change. Integration across time requires attention to whether coverage is constant.










WarningThe Invisible Error Problem



The most dangerous errors in data integration are the ones that produce no obvious error messages. A join that silently drops unmatched records, a merge that produces many-to-many duplications, a temporal alignment that misassigns observations to the wrong period — all of these can produce analytically plausible results that are systematically wrong. Spot-checking merged data against known cases and original sources is essential validation.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Problem: Understanding Future Energy Markets
Working with those near you: identify 10 variables you would include in a model of future energy markets. For each variable, specify:

The variable name
Its type (quantitative/qualitative) and scale of measurement (nominal/ordinal/interval/ratio/binary)
The data source you would use to operationalize it
Whether it is a direct measure or a proxy, and if a proxy, what concept it is proxying

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: Case 1: Measuring Democracy — A Measurement War
text:
Case 1: Measuring Democracy — A Measurement War
“Democracy” is one of the most consequential variables in international relations research — and one of the most contested to operationalize. Three major indices operationalize the same concept in fundamentally different ways and regularly produce conflicting results.










Index
Producer
Approach
Scale
Known Issues




Polity V
Center for Systemic Peace
Codes executive recruitment, constraints on executive, and political competition
−10 (autocracy) to +10 (democracy)
Coders sometimes disagree significantly; transitions poorly captured


Freedom House
Freedom House (U.S. NGO)
Rates political rights and civil liberties separately; explicitly normative framing
1–7 on each dimension; aggregate Free/Partly Free/Not Free
Critics argue ratings are influenced by U.S. foreign policy interests


V-Dem
V-Dem Institute, University of Gothenburg
Multi-dimensional: measures electoral, liberal, participatory, deliberative, and egalitarian democracy separately
0–1 on each component index
Less tested for very recent events









WarningMunck and Verkuilen’s Critique



In a widely-cited 2002 article, Munck and Verkuilen evaluated twelve democracy indices on conceptualization, measurement, and aggregation. They found that no existing index performed well on all three dimensions — each had significant methodological vulnerabilities. Any analyst using a democracy index should read this critique before treating scores as ground truth.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: Case 2: Conflict Counting — ACLED vs. UCDP
text:
Case 2: Conflict Counting — ACLED vs. UCDP


ACLED

Unit: individual events (battles, violence, protests, riots)
Threshold: any organized political violence — no minimum fatality threshold
Strength: captures low-intensity conflict; near real-time
Limitation: over-represents media-accessible areas



UCDP

Unit: conflict dyads (organized actor vs. organized actor)
Threshold: 25 battle-related deaths per year minimum
Strength: rigorous definitional consistency; long historical coverage (1946–present)
Limitation: misses sub-threshold violence; annual release cycle



Studies using UCDP will show fewer conflicts than studies using ACLED for the same country-year. Neither is “wrong” — they measure different things. An analyst must select based on whether their question is about organized armed conflict above a fatality threshold or any politically motivated violence.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
What Is a Data Dictionary?
A data dictionary documents every field in a dataset: its name, data type, allowed values, definition, and source. Without it, a dataset is almost impossible to use correctly — especially when received from another organization.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: Example: A Conflict Events Data Dictionary
text:
Example: A Conflict Events Data Dictionary










Field Name
Data Type
Scale
Allowed Values
Definition




event_id
Integer
—
Unique integer
System-generated primary key


country_code
String (3)
Nominal
ISO alpha-3
ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code


event_date
Date
Interval
YYYY-MM-DD
Date the event occurred (UTC)


event_type
String
Nominal
Battle / VAC / Protest / Riot / Explosion
Type of political violence

0–99999; −9 = unknown
Reported deaths; −9 if unknown

NoteThe Data Dictionary as an Analytical Contract



When you receive data from a partner agency or academic database, the data dictionary defines what you are actually getting. Missing or ambiguous dictionaries are among the most common sources of analytical error.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Spreadsheets
Spreadsheets (Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets) are the most accessible computational tool: a visual grid for entering, cleaning, computing, and charting small tabular datasets, with formulas and pivot tables for quick summaries. Their strength is immediacy; their weakness is scale and reproducibility. As the Reinhart–Rogoff error (this week’s required reading) demonstrates, an unseen formula mistake in a spreadsheet can propagate silently into a consequential conclusion. Spreadsheets do not scale to millions of rows and leave no clear audit trail of what was done.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Relational Databases and SQL
A relational database stores structured data across linked tables (Part II). SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for retrieving and combining that data. Rather than scrolling through a table, the analyst describes the records they want and the database returns them.
SELECT country, year, battle_deaths
FROM   conflict_events
WHERE  region = 'Sahel'
  AND  year >= 2015
ORDER BY battle_deaths DESC;
This query asks for three fields, filtered to one region and time window, sorted by severity. A JOIN combines tables on a shared key — for example, attaching economic indicators to each conflict record:
SELECT e.country, e.battle_deaths, c.gdp_per_capita
FROM   conflict_events AS e
JOIN   country_indicators AS c
  ON   e.country = c.country AND e.year = c.year
WHERE  e.year = 2020;
SQL is how analysts work with IC data warehouses and large relational datasets — it turns a structural relationship between tables (Part II’s keys) into an analytical result.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Programming Languages
For anything beyond a single query — statistical modeling, automation, reproducible workflows, machine learning — analysts turn to general-purpose languages. R (with the tidyverse and ggplot2) is built for statistics, exploratory data analysis, and visualization. Python (with pandas, NumPy, and scikit-learn) is a general-purpose language strong in automation, machine learning, text processing, and API access. Julia targets high-performance numerical computing. Their shared advantage over spreadsheets is reproducibility: the analysis is written as code that can be reviewed, rerun, and audited — the computational embodiment of the data dictionary’s “analytical contract” (Part X).

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)
An API is a defined interface that lets one program request data or services from another. Instead of manually downloading files, an analyst sends a structured request to a remote system and receives structured data — usually JSON — in return.
GET https://api.worldbank.org/v2/country/IR/indicator/NY.GDP.MKTP.CD?format=json
This request retrieves Iran’s GDP series from the World Bank’s public API. APIs are the backbone of programmatic OSINT collection (Week 2): social-media platforms, financial data providers, flight- and vessel-tracking services, and government open-data portals all expose APIs that let analysts pull current data automatically and repeatably rather than by hand.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:

TipA Pattern Worth Noticing



Read down the “Type of Data” column and the course’s logic reappears: the scale of measurement and structure of your data (Parts I–II) largely dictate which tool is appropriate. Tabular data goes to spreadsheets, databases, R, or Python; relational data to SQL; network data to Gephi or NetworkX; spatial data to GIS. Choosing a tool is, in part, a statement about what kind of data you believe you have.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text: Models are deliberate simplifications that make data interpretable. This lecture covers the principal modeling frameworks available to intelligence analysts: from regression and classification to model evaluation metrics. The emphasis is on matching the right model to the analytical question, evaluating model performance rigorously, and understanding the assumptions and failure modes of each approach. The lecture concludes with a conceptual bridge to machine learning, covered in depth in Lecture 10.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Why Model?

“All models are wrong, but some are useful.” — George Box

Data is never the world itself—it is a trace of the world, filtered through collection processes that introduce their own noise and distortions. Models are deliberate simplifications that make data interpretable.
There are two fundamental sources of uncertainty in any dataset:

Process randomness: the underlying phenomenon itself contains randomness (e.g., when exactly a volcano will erupt)
Measurement uncertainty: our instruments, sensors, and collection methods introduce noise and error

A statistical model is a mathematical function that separates signal from noise—it represents the systematic patterns in the data while acknowledging that residual variation exists and is unmodeled.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
section: Match the Model to the Problem
text:
Match the Model to the Problem
Different problems require different modeling approaches. The nature of your outcome variable (dependent variable) and the goal of the analysis are the primary determinants:









Approach
Goal
DV Type
Examples




Regression
Predict a continuous outcome
Continuous/Ratio
Estimating GDP, forecasting temperature, predicting casualties


Classification
Predict a category
Nominal/Binary
Spam vs. not spam; conflict onset vs. no conflict; weapon type identification


Clustering
Discover groups
None (unsupervised)
Segmenting states by behavior, detecting anomalous financial accounts


Time Series Analysis
Analyze ordered temporal data
Continuous over time
Stock prices, conflict event rates, satellite signal patterns

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Variable Type and Model Selection







DV Variable Type
Appropriate Statistical Models




Nominal
Chi-square tests, Logistic Regression (Multinomial), Log-linear models


Ordinal
Ordinal Logistic Regression, Non-parametric tests (Kruskal-Wallis, Mann-Whitney U), Cumulative link models


Interval / Ratio
Linear Regression, ANOVA, Multiple Regression, Factor Analysis, ANCOVA


Continuous
Linear Regression, GLM, Pearson’s Correlation, T-tests

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
What is Multiple Regression?
Multiple regression models the relationship between one dependent variable (DV) and two or more independent variables (IVs). The goal is to build a statistical model that:

Predicts values of the DV based on observed IVs
Estimates the contribution of each IV while controlling for the others
Measures effects in a way that isolates each variable’s unique contribution

The general linear model:
\[Y = \alpha + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_k X_k + \varepsilon\]
where \(\alpha\) is the intercept, each \(\beta_i\) is the slope (partial regression coefficient) for variable \(X_i\), and \(\varepsilon\) is the residual error.






NoteInterpreting Partial Regression Coefficients



\(\beta_i\) represents the expected unit change in Y for a one-unit increase in \(X_i\), holding all other variables constant. This “net regression coefficient” is the fundamental advantage of multiple regression over simple regression: it controls for the influence of other variables.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Regression Modeling Steps

Define the problem or question
Specify the model (select IVs based on theory and prior research)
Collect and clean data
Conduct descriptive data analysis (EDA)
Estimate model parameters (using OLS, MLE, or another estimator)
Evaluate model fit and assumptions
Use model for prediction or inference

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Design Requirements

One dependent variable (criterion)
Two or more independent variables (predictors)
Sample size: at minimum 50 observations, with at least 10 cases per independent variable (so 10 IVs → need 100+ observations)

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Assumptions



Independence

Observations are independent of each other. Violated by time series data, clustered data (students within schools), or repeated measures without correction.

Normality

In the population, the dependent variable is approximately normally distributed for each combination of IV values. Less critical with large samples due to the Central Limit Theorem.

Homoscedasticity

The variance of residuals is constant across all levels of the IVs. Violated when errors are larger for some values of X than others (e.g., variance in income increases at higher income levels).

Linearity

The relationship between the DV and each IV is linear when all other IVs are held constant. Non-linear relationships require polynomial terms or non-linear models.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Evaluating Model Fit

R²: The proportion of variance in Y explained by the model. R² = 0.60 means the model accounts for 60% of variation in the outcome.
Adjusted R²: Penalizes for adding variables that do not improve fit. Always use adjusted R² when comparing models with different numbers of predictors.
F-test (ANOVA table): Tests whether the overall model explains significantly more variance than a null model with no predictors.
t-tests for individual coefficients: Tests whether each \(\beta_i\) differs significantly from zero.
Residual analysis: Plot residuals vs. fitted values (checking homoscedasticity), Q-Q plot (checking normality), residuals vs. each IV (detecting nonlinearity).

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Multicollinearity
Multicollinearity occurs when IVs are highly correlated with each other. Consequences:

Coefficient estimates become unstable and sensitive to which variables are included
Standard errors inflate, reducing statistical power
Interpretation becomes difficult—coefficients reflect the combined effect of correlated predictors

Detection: Examine the correlation matrix between IVs. Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) > 10 indicates problematic multicollinearity.
Solutions: remove one of the correlated variables; combine them into a composite index; collect new data with greater variation in predictors.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
section: Why Not Linear Regression for Binary Outcomes?
text:
Why Not Linear Regression for Binary Outcomes?
When the dependent variable is binary (0/1, Yes/No), linear regression produces predictions outside the [0,1] probability range and violates normality and homoscedasticity assumptions. The solution is logistic regression.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
The Logistic (Sigmoid) Function
Logistic regression models the log-odds of the outcome as a linear function of the IVs:
\[\ln\left(\frac{P(Y=1)}{1-P(Y=1)}\right) = \alpha + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_k X_k\]
The predicted log-odds are then transformed back to a probability through the logistic function:
\[P(Y=1) = \frac{e^{\alpha + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots}}{1 + e^{\alpha + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots}}\]
This constrains predictions to (0, 1) regardless of IV values—solving the out-of-range problem.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Interpreting Coefficients
Logistic regression coefficients are in log-odds units. A coefficient of \(\beta_i = 1.48\) for a binary variable means: - The log-odds of \(Y=1\) increase by 1.48 for a one-unit increase in \(X_i\) - Equivalently: the odds of \(Y=1\) are multiplied by \(e^{1.48} \approx 4.39\) for a one-unit increase in \(X_i\)

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Model Comparison
For logistic regression, model comparison uses the likelihood-ratio test rather than changes in R²: - Compute the log-likelihood for two nested models - The difference in log-likelihoods follows a chi-square distribution - A statistically significant improvement in log-likelihood justifies including the added variable

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Multinomial Logistic Regression
When the outcome has more than two categories (e.g., preferred mode of transport: car, bus, train, bicycle), multinomial logistic regression extends the binary case by estimating the log-odds of each category relative to a chosen baseline.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
ML vs. Statistical Models


Statistical Models

Goal: understand the underlying generative process
“White box” — coefficients have interpretable meaning
Concerned with uncertainty quantification (standard errors, confidence intervals)
Better for causal inference and hypothesis testing
Output includes inference about parameters and their significance



Machine Learning Algorithms

Goal: maximize predictive accuracy
Often “black box” — internal workings are opaque
Less concerned with understanding the generative process
Suited for high-dimensional data and large samples
Output is a prediction or classification, not a parameter estimate



The choice between statistical and ML approaches is not “which is better” — it depends on whether the analytical goal is understanding (statistical modeling) or prediction (ML). Intelligence analysts often need both: ML to flag anomalies at scale, statistical modeling to explain and justify findings to consumers.






TipWhen to Use Each



Use statistical regression when: the outcome has clear theoretical predictors, sample sizes are moderate, and the consumer needs to understand why the model predicts what it predicts.
Use machine learning when: prediction accuracy is the primary goal, the feature space is large and complex, labeled training data is available, and interpretability can be traded for performance.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
section: Beyond Accuracy: How to Evaluate a Classifier
text:
Beyond Accuracy: How to Evaluate a Classifier
When a model predicts a binary outcome (conflict/no-conflict; threat/no-threat; spam/not-spam), accuracy — the percentage of correct predictions — is often a misleading metric. A model that predicts “no attack” for every observation in a terrorism dataset would be highly accurate (since attacks are rare) but analytically worthless.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
The Confusion Matrix
The confusion matrix maps predicted vs. actual outcomes:









Predicted Positive
Predicted Negative




Actual Positive
True Positive (TP)
False Negative (FN) — Type II Error


Actual Negative
False Positive (FP) — Type I Error
True Negative (TN)



From these four cells, the key metrics:








Metric
Formula
Intelligence Meaning




Accuracy
(TP + TN) / Total
Overall correct predictions


Precision
TP / (TP + FP)
When model says “attack,” how often is it right?


Recall (Sensitivity)
TP / (TP + FN)
Of all actual attacks, how many did the model catch?


F1 Score
2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall)
Harmonic mean of precision and recall


Specificity
TN / (TN + FP)
Of actual negatives, how many did the model correctly classify?



The precision-recall tradeoff:
Lowering the classification threshold (flagging more cases as positive) increases recall but decreases precision — more true threats caught, more false alarms generated. Raising the threshold does the reverse. The optimal balance depends entirely on the cost ratio between false positives and false negatives — a decision that must be made by the analyst and consumer, not the algorithm.






WarningIntelligence-Specific Threshold Setting



In an intelligence context, the cost of a false negative (missing an actual attack) vastly exceeds the cost of a false positive (investigating an innocent party) for most applications. This asymmetry should drive threshold setting toward high recall / lower precision. But there are contexts where false positives carry very high costs — air strike targeting, no-fly list additions, lethal autonomous weapons systems. Threshold setting is an ethical and policy decision, not a technical one.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
The ROC Curve and AUC
The Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve plots True Positive Rate (recall) against False Positive Rate (1 − specificity) at every possible threshold. The Area Under the Curve (AUC) summarizes model performance across all thresholds:

AUC = 1.0: perfect classifier
AUC = 0.5: no better than random
AUC < 0.5: worse than random (predictions are inverted)

AUC is threshold-independent, making it useful for comparing models before deciding where to set the operational threshold.

title: Week 8: Data Modeling: Regression and Model Evaluation
text:
Train-Test Split and Cross-Validation
A model that performs well on the data it was trained on may perform poorly on new data — the overfitting problem. Rigorous model evaluation requires evaluation on data the model has not seen.
Standard practices:


Train-test split
Divide data into training set (typically 70–80%) and held-out test set (20–30%). Train the model on the training set; evaluate performance only on the test set. Never use the test set for model development decisions — only for final evaluation.
Validation set
If hyperparameter tuning is needed, add a third partition (validation set) between training and final testing. Training set: fit; validation set: tune; test set: evaluate.


k-Fold Cross-Validation
Divide data into k equal folds. Train on k−1 folds, evaluate on the held-out fold. Repeat k times (each fold serves as the test set once). Average performance across folds. More robust than a single split, particularly with small datasets.
Temporal holdout for time-series data
In conflict or financial forecasting, always hold out the most recent observations for testing — never random observations. Future data cannot be used to predict past events; temporal ordering must be preserved.
